executiveSummary:
## Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

National Medicaid enrollment continues to normalize following the end of the public health emergency, with total enrollment of 75.3 million across 51 reporting states as of January 2026. Most major states are experiencing enrollment declines, with Michigan showing the steepest decrease at -7.6% year-over-year, followed by California and Illinois at -6.2% each. Texas mirrors Pennsylvania's decline at -3.1%, positioning it in the middle range of enrollment changes among large states. Only North Carolina among the top ten states showed enrollment growth at +0.5%. From a fiscal perspective, national per-enrollee spending reached $2,869 in fiscal year 2024, with total expenditures of $215.9 billion and federal funding comprising $142.0 billion of program costs.

Quality performance remains highly variable across states, with the national average score of 65.6 out of 100 highlighting significant room for improvement. Texas ranks 30th nationally with a quality score of 62, placing it in the moderate performance tier but below the national average. This contrasts sharply with top performers like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80), while states like Mississippi (48) and Oklahoma (51) continue to struggle with quality outcomes. Texas demonstrates strong managed care penetration at 93.3%, well above the national average of 83.2%, covering nearly 3.9 million enrollees through managed care arrangements.

State leadership should note that recent federal regulatory activity indicates potential enrollment moratoria and enhanced oversight measures, as evidenced by multiple Federal Register announcements in May 2026. Texas's per-enrollee costs of $2,988 remain reasonable compared to high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Massachusetts ($4,201), though the state's quality performance suggests opportunities for targeted improvements to move closer to national benchmarks and peer state performance levels.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a significant but manageable -3.1% year-over-year decline that aligns closely with national unwinding trends and matches Pennsylvania's reduction rate. At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas maintains spending discipline that falls between the national average ($2,869) and higher-cost states like New York ($3,431) and Ohio ($3,561), while significantly outpacing Florida's notably lean $2,031 per-enrollee rate. Among non-expansion southern peers, Texas demonstrates cost efficiency compared to states with similar demographics, though this restraint may contribute to its middling quality performance at 62/100 (ranking #30 nationally), which places it above struggling southern states like Arkansas (54) and Alabama (52) but well below high-performing programs.

The state's standout strength lies in its managed care infrastructure, with 93.3% penetration rate significantly exceeding the 83.2% national average, suggesting sophisticated care coordination capabilities that position Texas favorably for value-based initiatives. However, key challenges persist around quality improvement—the state's moderate tier ranking indicates substantial room for enhancement in care outcomes and member experience. Areas to monitor include how effectively Texas leverages its strong managed care platform to drive quality gains, whether per-enrollee spending pressures will intensify as post-pandemic enrollment stabilizes, and how the state navigates federal regulatory changes around provider enrollment moratoria that could impact network adequacy. The combination of operational scale, cost management, and managed care maturity provides a solid foundation, but translating these structural advantages into measurably better health outcomes remains the critical next phase for Texas Medicaid.

alerts.title: PA Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: MI Enrollment Declined 7.6% Year-Over-Year
alerts.description: Michigan experienced a 7.6% decrease in Medicaid enrollment, significantly above the typical decline threshold of 10% but still notable.

alerts.title: Bottom 5 States Show Critical Quality Performance
alerts.description: Arkansas, South Carolina, Alabama, Oklahoma, and Mississippi have quality scores below 55, indicating serious care quality concerns.

alerts.title: FL Low Per-Enrollee Spending May Signal Access Issues
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below national average, potentially indicating inadequate service provision or access barriers.

alerts.title: Federal Moratorium on Provider Enrollment Active
alerts.description: CMS has announced nationwide temporary moratoriums on provider enrollment which may impact program operations and access.

executiveInsights.title: CMS announces nationwide temporary enrollment moratorium affecting TX
executiveInsights.summary: Federal Register shows CMS announced nationwide temporary moratoriums on Medicaid enrollment in May 2026. This directly impacts Texas's 4.03M enrollee base and ongoing operations.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment moratoriums fundamentally change Medicaid operations, system requirements, and contract scope. Accenture must immediately assess impact on Texas deliverables and system capacity planning.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag enrollment moratorium to HHSC leadership and convene emergency contract impact assessment

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score ranks #30 nationally at 62/100, underperforming
executiveInsights.summary: Texas scores 62/100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally and falling in moderate tier. National average is 65.6 with 10 states achieving high performance (≥75).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Poor quality performance creates regulatory risk and potential CMS intervention. This directly impacts contract performance metrics and may trigger remediation requirements under the Accenture engagement.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review quality improvement initiatives with HHSC and benchmark against top 5 performers

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs $2,988 vs national average $2,869
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee compared to national average of $2,869, with total expenditures of $12.0B. However, costs remain lower than comparable large states like NY ($3,431) and PA ($4,377).
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Above-average per-enrollee costs indicate potential inefficiencies or higher acuity populations. Understanding cost drivers is critical for contract optimization and budget forecasting.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Analyze Texas cost drivers compared to FL ($2,031) and IL ($2,715) for optimization opportunities

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% YoY to 4.03M members
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% year-over-year to 4,032,864 enrollees, consistent with national declining enrollment trends. Only NC showed growth at +0.5% among top 10 states.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Declining enrollment affects contract revenue, system utilization, and operational planning. Accenture must adjust capacity planning and help HHSC understand if this is unwinding-related or structural decline.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review enrollment forecasting models with HHSC and assess impact on system capacity planning

executiveInsights.title: CMS releases 2028 HCBS quality measures impacting managed care operations
executiveInsights.summary: New Federal Register notice establishes 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services quality measure set. Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration (3.86M enrollees) that will be affected.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: New HCBS quality measures will require system modifications and reporting changes for Texas's extensive managed care network. Early preparation is critical for compliance and competitive positioning.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality requirements and assess system modification needs for managed care reporting

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline acceleration
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment declining 3.1% YoY with potential for steeper drops affecting system volumes and economics.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop flexible capacity models to maintain margins during enrollment volatility.

riskOpportunity.label: Quality score improvement
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks 30th nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating significant improvement opportunity.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Propose comprehensive quality improvement initiative targeting top-10 state performance.

riskOpportunity.label: Provider enrollment moratorium
riskOpportunity.description: CMS nationwide temporary moratorium on provider enrollment creates operational complexity and compliance risks.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated compliance team for moratorium navigation and exception processing.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality measures
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services quality requirements create system modernization needs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position as lead vendor for HCBS quality measurement system implementation.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care recompete
riskOpportunity.description: Texas 93.3% managed care penetration suggests upcoming MCO contract recompetes affecting system requirements.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Engage early with HHSC on managed care technology roadmap and vendor selection criteria.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas per-enrollee costs at $2,988 versus national $2,869 create budget optimization pressures.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop cost reduction analytics platform highlighting administrative savings opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio oversight creates audit risk but also positions compliance expertise as valuable.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen audit readiness protocols and market compliance consulting capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Procurement pipeline expansion
riskOpportunity.description: Texas HHSC active procurement portal indicates growing contracting opportunities beyond current scope.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish systematic monitoring and pursuit strategy for HHSC procurement opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, reflecting post-pandemic unwinding trends.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee annually, below the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration, significantly above the national average of 83.2%.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas scores 62 out of 100 on quality measures, ranking 30th nationally in moderate tier.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Specific count not available, but Texas HHSC and ESBD portals show active procurement opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Twelve active regulatory and oversight items tracked across HHS OIG and Federal Register sources.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones identified in current data sources.
